Skeleton (XbView)

The Skeleton structure represents a hierarchy of frame nodes. It also implements the functionality by which the hierarchy is updated and concatenated together with any animation data. Each resource file can have multiple skeletons defined. Indeed, for skinned animation there are usually three separate hierarchies:

  1. The skin mesh itself in its own object space.
  2. The hierarchy of bones needed to animate the skin.
  3. The hierarchy of bone inverse rest matrices used to construct the bone matrices used for skinning. These are in a separate hierarchy because they come pre-concatenated and inverted from Deep Exploration, and we don't want to do anything to them. 
struct Skeleton 
{
    DWORD    m_FrameCount; // total number of frames
    XGMATRIX *m_rMatrix;   // cumulative transformations computed from 
                           // frame hierarchy in depth-first traversal
    Frame    *m_pRoot;     // root of frame hierarchy
	
    //------------------------------------------------------------------------
    // Set root of frame hierarchy and allocate matrix array
    // to hold one matrix per node in the hierarchy. 
    HRESULT SetRoot(Frame *pRoot);

    //------------------------------------------------------------------------
    // Do depth-first traversal of frame hierarchy to compute the
    // matrix palette.
    HRESULT UpdateTransformations( XBR *pXBR = NULL, FLOAT fTimeStamp = 0.0f, 
                                   BOOL bInterpolate = FALSE );
	
    //------------------------------------------------------------------------
    // Find the bounding box of the frames (does not include any mesh 
    // geometry). 
    // Uses current matrix palette in m_rMatrix as computed by 
    // UpdateTransformations. 
    HRESULT ComputeBoundingBox(XGVECTOR3 *pvMin, XGVECTOR3 *pvMax);

    XGMATRIX *GetFrameMatrix( const CHAR *strFrameId );
};

The m_FrameCount member tells us how many frame nodes are in this hierarchy; m_rMatrix is an array of matrices holding the results of the concatenation done by the UpdateTransformations function. The m_pRoot pointer points to the first frame node in the hierarchy. By using the m_pChild and m_pNext pointers in the root node, one can traverse the entire hierarchy.

The SetRoot function sets the m_pRoot pointer and allocates the m_rMatrix array to hold the concatenation. The UpdateTransformations function concatenates the frame hierarchy together and stores the results at each node. This function also concatenates in the animation data, if present, with each node in the hierarchy. The function starts at the root node of the hierarchy and traverses the hierarchy concatenating the matrices as it goes. UpdateTransformations also calls XBR::GetAnimationData for each node as it traverses the hierarchy. If the animation data exists, GetAnimationData returns S_OK, which tells UpdateTransformations to concatenate in the animation data. The animation data is returned as a set of matrices containing rotation, translation, and scale data. The final matrix with animation data is concatenated according to this formula:

mRotation * mScale * pFrame->m_Matrix * mTranslation

Since matrix multiplication is not commutative, you cannot create a simple animation matrix by multiplying the rotation, scale, and translation together and then multiplying that by the frame. In other words:

( mRotation * mScale * mTranslation ) * pFrame->m_Matrix !=
	mRotation * mScale * pFrame->m_Matrix * mTranslation)

Each node in the hierarchy is limited to a maximum number of children and siblings. That maximum number is defined at 200 at present. ComputeBoundingBox calculates the bounding box that would contain all of the frame nodes. It does not take into account any of the geometry. GetFrameMatrix returns the concatenated matrix for the Frame node named by the strFrameId passed in.
